questions.q: Explain the trends in the oxidising properties of elements in group 16 of the periodic table. Give reasons for the trends observed.
questions.answer: The oxidising properties of elements in group 16 of the periodic table decrease from top to bottom. This is because the elements at the top of the group have a higher electronegativity and a smaller size, which enables them to attract electrons more easily and exhibit stronger oxidising properties. As we move down the group, the size of the elements increases and their electronegativity decreases, resulting in weaker oxidising properties.

questions.q: What is the structure of the molecule ClF3? Explain how the bond angle and molecular geometry are determined.
questions.answer: The molecule ClF3 has a T-shaped structure. The bond angle and molecular geometry are determined by the VSEPR theory, which states that electron pairs around a central atom will arrange themselves to minimize repulsions. In ClF3, the central chlorine atom has three bonding pairs and two lone pairs, resulting in a trigonal bipyramidal electron geometry and a T-shaped molecular geometry.
marking_scheme: Application of VSEPR theory to ClF3 (1 mark)

questions.q: Describe the preparation and properties of sulphuric acid. How is it used in the manufacture of fertilisers?
questions.answer: Sulphuric acid is prepared by the contact process, which involves the oxidation of sulphur dioxide to sulphur trioxide, followed by the reaction of sulphur trioxide with water. Sulphuric acid is a strong acid and a dehydrating agent, and it is used in the manufacture of fertilisers such as ammonium sulphate and superphosphate.

questions.q: A sample of white phosphorus (P4) weighs 3.8 g. Calculate the number of moles of phosphorus present. (Atomic mass of phosphorus = 31 g mol^-1)
questions.answer:
Calculation: 
Number of moles = mass of phosphorus / molar mass of phosphorus 
= 3.8 g / 31 g mol^-1 
= 0.123 mol 
Number of moles of phosphorus present = 0.123 mol

questions.q: A 25 mL solution of sodium thiosulphate (Na2S2O3) is required to react completely with 30 mL of 0.1 M iodine solution. Calculate the molarity of the sodium thiosulphate solution. The reaction is: 2Na2S2O3 + I2 → Na2S4O6 + 2NaI
questions.answer:
Calculation: 
Number of moles of iodine = molarity x volume 
= 0.1 M x (30/1000) L 
= 0.003 mol 
From the equation, 1 mol of iodine reacts with 2 mol of sodium thiosulphate. 
Number of moles of sodium thiosulphate required = 2 x 0.003 mol 
= 0.006 mol 
Molarity of sodium thiosulphate = number of moles / volume 
= 0.006 mol / (25/1000) L 
= 0.24 M

questions.q: The standard enthalpy of formation of SF6 is -1209 kJ mol^-1. Calculate the bond energy of the S-F bond in SF6, given that the standard enthalpy of formation of sulphur is 0 kJ mol^-1 and that of fluorine is 0 kJ mol^-1.
questions.answer:
Calculation: 
Standard enthalpy of formation of SF6 = Σ(standard enthalpy of formation of products) - Σ(standard enthalpy of formation of reactants) 
-1209 kJ mol^-1 = [SF6] - [S] - 6[F2] 
Since the standard enthalpy of formation of sulphur and fluorine are 0 kJ mol^-1, 
-1209 kJ mol^-1 = [SF6] - 6[F2] 
The bond energy of the S-F bond in SF6 = standard enthalpy of formation of SF6 / 6 
= -1209 kJ mol^-1 / 6 
= -201.5 kJ mol^-1
